title: Access Control and Identity Management Policy
content:
Access Control and Identity Management Policy v3.1 — Aligned with NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 AC family, DoD Zero Trust Reference Architecture, and CIS Controls.

1. PRINCIPLE OF LEAST PRIVILEGE
1.1 All access to systems, applications, and data must be granted based on the principle of least privilege — users receive only the minimum permissions required to perform their job function.
1.2 Default access for new accounts must be no-access. Permissions must be explicitly requested and approved.
1.3 Access must be role-based (RBAC). Direct permission grants to individual users are prohibited except where RBAC cannot be applied.
1.4 Standing privileged access is prohibited where just-in-time (JIT) access solutions are available. Privileged access must be requested per-session with automatic expiration.

2. ACCOUNT LIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT
2.1 User accounts must be provisioned through an approved identity management system integrated with HR onboarding.
2.2 Account provisioning requires manager approval and, for access to sensitive systems, data owner approval.
2.3 Accounts for terminated employees must be disabled within 4 hours of termination and deleted within 30 days.
2.4 Accounts for contractors and temporary staff must have a defined expiration date, not exceeding the contract end date.
2.5 Accounts inactive for 45 days must be automatically disabled. Accounts inactive for 90 days must be automatically deleted.
2.6 Quarterly access reviews must be conducted for all systems containing Confidential or Restricted data. Reviews must be completed within 30 days and documented.

3. PRIVILEGED ACCESS MANAGEMENT
3.1 All privileged accounts (domain admin, root, database admin, cloud admin) must be managed through the enterprise Privileged Access Management (PAM) solution.
3.2 Privileged sessions must be recorded and stored for a minimum of 1 year.
3.3 Shared administrative accounts are prohibited. Each administrator must use a unique, named privileged account separate from their standard user account.
3.4 Privileged accounts must not be used for routine activities (email, web browsing, document editing).
3.5 Emergency break-glass accounts must be stored in a sealed, audited physical or digital vault with dual-custody access.

4. SERVICE AND APPLICATION ACCOUNTS
4.1 Service accounts must be inventoried with documented owner, purpose, and systems accessed.
4.2 Service accounts must use managed service account (gMSA/sMSA) features where supported.
4.3 Service accounts must not have interactive login capability.
4.4 Service account permissions must be reviewed semi-annually by the application owner.
4.5 Service account credentials must be stored in an enterprise secrets vault (HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) — never in source code, configuration files, or scripts.

5. REMOTE AND THIRD-PARTY ACCESS
5.1 Third-party vendor access must be provisioned through a dedicated vendor access portal with time-limited sessions.
5.2 Vendor access must be monitored in real-time with session recording for access to sensitive systems.
5.3 Vendor accounts must be disabled when not actively in use and reviewed monthly.
5.4 VPN-based remote access requires MFA (per SEC-002) and device compliance verification.
5.5 Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA) is the preferred model for remote access to applications.

6. AUTHENTICATION STANDARDS
6.1 All systems must enforce the Enterprise Password Policy (SEC-001) and MFA Policy (SEC-002).
6.2 Single Sign-On (SSO) via SAML 2.0 or OpenID Connect must be implemented for all enterprise applications.
6.3 Local application accounts (bypassing SSO) are prohibited except for break-glass scenarios.
6.4 Authentication events (success and failure) must be logged centrally with user ID, timestamp, source IP, and application.

7. AUTHORIZATION AND ENTITLEMENT
7.1 Application entitlements must be mapped to business roles maintained in the identity governance platform.
7.2 Segregation of duties (SoD) must be enforced — no single user may have permissions that create a conflict of interest (e.g., both requestor and approver roles).
7.3 Elevated permissions granted for troubleshooting or project work must automatically expire within 8 hours unless renewed.

8. REFERENCES
- NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 AC-1 through AC-25
- NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust Architecture
- DoD Zero Trust Reference Architecture v2.0
- CIS Controls v8 Controls 5.1-5.6, 6.1-6.8
- ISO 27001:2022 Annex A.9
